narrative:
Markets are in a business-as-usual regime with a stress score of 18, reflecting genuine calm rather than complacency. SPY has barely moved over five days, down just 0.1 percent with a negligible z-score, and only one of eleven sectors is trading in red, indicating broad-based stability and no sign of rolling deterioration.

The VIX sits at 17.4, which ranks at the 42nd percentile of its ten-year range—materially higher than crisis lows but well below any alert threshold. The term structure remains in normal contango, suggesting no forward-looking anxiety among options traders. High yield spreads at 279 basis points are exceptionally tight at the 14th percentile over five years, while investment-grade spreads at 76 basis points are similarly compressed; credit is confirming the equity calm decisively, with both measures showing substantial tightening relative to history.

Chinese equities are the notable outlier, with the FXI index down 5.3 percent in five days and three of eight emerging market indices trading red. Materials have also underperformed, falling 4.5 percent, likely reflecting both China exposure and broader commodity headwinds. Energy is the clear winner at plus 3.8 percent alongside German equities and financials, suggesting a selective rotation toward real assets and cyclical value rather than any panic reallocation.

No equity dislocations have triggered, and no deep value opportunities are currently flagged by the screening framework.
